title: Multimodal LLMs — Vision, Audio & Video at Scale
description: Multimodal LLMs process text, images, audio, and video in a single model. At FAANG Staff+ level, you must design systems that handle high-throughput vision pipelines, streaming audio transcription with diarization, video frame sampling strategies, and cost-efficient multimodal caching — across providers with different modality support and tokenisation schemes.

sections.title: Multimodal Architecture: How LLMs See and Hear
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: Multimodal LLMs extend text-only transformers with modality encoders that project images, audio, and video into the LLM's embedding space. Vision: a Vision Transformer (ViT) or CLIP encoder splits images into patches, embeds them, and projects via a learned adapter into the LLM's token space. The LLM attends to image patch tokens just like text tokens. Audio: raw audio is converted to spectrograms or encoded via Whisper/Conformer, then projected as token sequences. Video: treated as a sequence of frames (images) with temporal position embeddings. The Staff-level insight: cross-modal alignment is the hard part — the model must learn which image patches correspond to which text tokens, a problem solved by contrastive pre-training (CLIP) or generative pre-training (GPT-4V).

bar_chart Concrete numbers: GPT-4V processes images at 85-170 tokens per 512x512 tile (depending on detail mode). Low-res mode: 85 tokens/image. High-res mode: up to 170 tiles x 170 tokens = ~28,900 tokens for a full-resolution image (cost ~$0.15/image at GPT-4o pricing). Gemini 1.5 Pro supports up to 1M tokens of multimodal context — ~1 hour of video or ~30,000 images. Claude 3 handles images up to 8K resolution, charges at 1,600 tokens/image for a standard photo. Audio transcription latency: Whisper large-v3 transcribes 1 minute of audio in ~2s (GPU), ~15s (CPU). Video frame processing: sampling 1 fps for a 10-min video = 600 frames, at 170 tokens/frame = 102,000 tokens (~$0.50 in context alone).

track_changes Practical Workflow: 1) Choose the right detail mode: low-res for charts/diagrams (text matters more than pixels), high-res for medical imaging or document OCR. 2) For documents, split multi-page PDFs into individual images, process with high-res mode, and chain pages in a single context window. 3) For video, use scene-detection sampling (one frame per scene change) instead of fixed-rate sampling (1 fps) — reduces tokens 10x with no quality loss. 4) For audio, use Whisper for transcription then feed text to the LLM — cheaper than native audio tokenisation. 5) Cache image embeddings: the same image sent multiple times re-encodes each time unless you cache the vision encoder output.

search Interview Angle: "Design a system that processes 10,000 product images/minute for automated catalog enrichment." The answer should cover: parallel image encoding (GPU batching), detail mode selection, text extraction (OCR), caching embeddings, and cost modelling at scale.
sections.codeBlock:
# Multimodal document processing pipeline
import base64, json, os, time
from pathlib import Path

class MultimodalProcessor:
    """
    Production pipeline for multimodal document processing.
    Handles PDFs, images, audio files with cost tracking.
    """
    def __init__(self, model="gpt-4o", detail="auto"):
        self.model = model
        self.detail = detail
        self.cache: dict[str, str] = {}  # image_hash -> encoded representation

    def encode_image(self, image_path: str) -> str:
        """Encode image to base64 with caching."""
        path_hash = str(os.path.getmtime(image_path)) + image_path
        if path_hash in self.cache:
            return self.cache[path_hash]
        with open(image_path, "rb") as f:
            encoded = base64.b64encode(f.read()).decode("utf-8")
        self.cache[path_hash] = encoded
        return encoded

    def make_vision_message(self, prompt: str, images: list[str]) -> dict:
        """Create a multimodal message with embedded images."""
        content = [{"type": "text", "text": prompt}]
        for img in images:
            b64 = self.encode_image(img)
            content.append({
                "type": "image_url",
                "image_url": {
                    "url": f"data:image/png;base64,{b64}",
                    "detail": self.detail
                }
            })
        return {"role": "user", "content": content}

    def estimate_vision_cost(self, num_images: int, detail: str) -> float:
        """Estimate OpenAI vision API cost."""
        tokens_per_image = 85 if detail == "low" else 170
        total_tokens = num_images * tokens_per_image
        if self.model == "gpt-4o":
            return (total_tokens / 1_000_000) * 2.50  # $2.50/1M input tokens
        return (total_tokens / 1_000_000) * 10.00  # GPT-4: $10/1M

    def process_pdf(self, pdf_path: str, prompt: str, dpi: int = 200):
        """
        Convert PDF pages to images, process with vision model.
        Uses scene-change detection to skip similar pages.
        """
        from pdf2image import convert_from_path
        pages = convert_from_path(pdf_path, dpi=dpi)
        
        # Deduplicate near-identical pages (headers, blanks)
        unique_pages = [pages[0]]
        for i in range(1, len(pages)):
            diff = self._page_difference(pages[i-1], pages[i])
            if diff > 0.05:  # >5% pixel difference
                unique_pages.append(pages[i])
        
        # Process in batches of 5 to manage context
        results = []
        for i in range(0, len(unique_pages), 5):
            batch = unique_pages[i:i+5]
            # Save temp files then process
            temp_paths = []
            for j, page in enumerate(batch):
                tmp = f"/tmp/page_{i+j}.png"
                page.save(tmp, "PNG")
                temp_paths.append(tmp)
            # API call would go here
            results.append(self.make_vision_message(prompt, temp_paths))
        return results

    def _page_difference(self, p1, p2) -> float:
        """Compute pixel-level difference ratio between two page images."""
        import numpy as np
        a1 = np.array(p1)
        a2 = np.array(p2)
        if a1.shape != a2.shape:
            return 1.0
        diff = np.mean(np.abs(a1.astype(float) - a2.astype(float)))
        return diff / 255.0

sections.title: Vision at Scale: Image Processing Pipelines
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: Vision LLMs are not general-purpose image classifiers — they are image+text reasoners. A single image can contain multiple objects, text, charts, faces, and spatial relationships. The model decides what to attend to based on the text prompt. This is both powerful and fragile: if the prompt doesn't explicitly direct attention, the model may miss critical details. The Staff insight: always pair images with a structured prompt that tells the model what to extract, where to look, and how to format the output. Treat vision prompts like you treat function calls — schema, description, examples.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: GPT-4o vision accuracy on standard benchmarks: ChartQA 81.5%, DocVQA 92.3%, TextVQA 82.1%. Claude 3.5 Sonnet: ChartQA 84.3%, DocVQA 91.7%. Gemini 1.5 Pro: ChartQA 79.8%, DocVQA 88.9%. Performance degrades with dense text (small font, low contrast, overlapping text). Image encoding time: ~500ms for a 1MB image (GPU), ~3s (CPU). Batch encoding: 16 images in parallel on GPU ~2s vs 8s sequentially. Throughput: GPT-4o vision API processes ~50 images/min per API key (rate limited). Saturation: latency increases 3x under concurrent load without connection pooling.

track_changes Practical Workflow: 1) Pre-process images before sending to the LLM: resize to max 2048px on the longest side (reduces tokens 4x with minimal quality loss), convert to JPEG at 85% quality (reduces size 10x vs PNG), auto-rotate and crop whitespace. 2) For OCR-heavy use cases, use a dedicated OCR engine (Tesseract, Azure Document Intelligence) first and send the extracted text alongside the image — reduces hallucinations. 3) Implement retry with a different detail mode: if low-res fails, retry with high-res. 4) Use structured output (JSON mode) for extraction results. 5) Monitor image token usage — vision costs can dominate the bill if unmonitored.

search Interview Angle: "How would you build a system that extracts structured data from 1M invoices/day?" Key points: pre-processing pipeline (resize, OCR), model selection (GPT-4o for accuracy, Gemini for cost), batching strategy, cost optimization (caching + lower detail for simple invoices), and quality assurance (sampling 1% for human review).
sections.codeBlock:
# Production vision pipeline with pre-processing
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional
import cv2, numpy as np

@dataclass
class ImagePreprocessingConfig:
    max_dimension: int = 2048
    jpeg_quality: int = 85
    auto_rotate: bool = True
    crop_whitespace: bool = True
    enhance_contrast: bool = False

class VisionPreprocessor:
    """
    Pre-process images to reduce token usage and improve accuracy.
    Compresses, crops, and enhances before sending to vision LLM.
    """
    def __init__(self, config: ImagePreprocessingConfig = None):
        self.config = config or ImagePreprocessingConfig()

    def process(self, image: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
        """Apply all pre-processing steps."""
        result = image.copy()
        if self.config.crop_whitespace:
            result = self._crop_whitespace(result)
        if self.config.auto_rotate:
            result = self._auto_rotate(result)
        result = self._resize_if_needed(result)
        if self.config.enhance_contrast:
            result = self._enhance_contrast(result)
        return result

    def _resize_if_needed(self, img: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
        h, w = img.shape[:2]
        if max(h, w) <= self.config.max_dimension:
            return img
        scale = self.config.max_dimension / max(h, w)
        new_w, new_h = int(w * scale), int(h * scale)
        return cv2.resize(img, (new_w, new_h), interpolation=cv2.INTER_AREA)

    def _crop_whitespace(self, img: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
        gray = cv2.cvtColor(img, cv2.COLOR_BGR2GRAY)
        _, thresh = cv2.threshold(gray, 240, 255, cv2.THRESH_BINARY_INV)
        coords = cv2.findNonZero(thresh)
        if coords is not None:
            x, y, w, h = cv2.boundingRect(coords)
            return img[y:y+h, x:x+w]
        return img

    def _auto_rotate(self, img: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
        """Detect and correct orientation using text lines."""
        gray = cv2.cvtColor(img, cv2.COLOR_BGR2GRAY)
        edges = cv2.Canny(gray, 50, 150)
        lines = cv2.HoughLines(edges, 1, np.pi/180, 200)
        if lines is None:
            return img
        angles = [line[0][1] for line in lines]
        median_angle = np.median(angles)
        angle_deg = np.degrees(median_angle) - 90
        if abs(angle_deg) > 0.5:
            h, w = img.shape[:2]
            center = (w // 2, h // 2)
            M = cv2.getRotationMatrix2D(center, angle_deg, 1.0)
            return cv2.warpAffine(img, M, (w, h))
        return img

    def _enhance_contrast(self, img: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
        lab = cv2.cvtColor(img, cv2.COLOR_BGR2LAB)
        l, a, b = cv2.split(lab)
        clahe = cv2.createCLAHE(clipLimit=3.0, tileGridSize=(8, 8))
        l = clahe.apply(l)
        enhanced = cv2.merge([l, a, b])
        return cv2.cvtColor(enhanced, cv2.COLOR_LAB2BGR)

    def compression_ratio(self, original: np.ndarray, processed: np.ndarray) -> float:
        """Compute compression ratio (lower = smaller)."""
        import sys
        orig_size = sys.getsizeof(original.tobytes())
        proc_size = sys.getsizeof(processed.tobytes())
        return proc_size / max(orig_size, 1)

sections.title: Audio & Video: Speech, Transcription & Temporal Reasoning
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: Audio and video processing introduces the temporal dimension — the model must reason about sequences of frames or audio samples, not static content. For audio, the dominant pattern is speech-to-text (Whisper) feeding into a text LLM. Native audio LLMs (Gemini 1.5, GPT-4o audio) process raw audio tokens, enabling emotion detection, speaker identification, and music understanding. For video, the key challenge is information density vs. cost: 1 second of video at 30fps is 30 images. No model can attend to all frames. The solution is smart frame sampling, scene detection, and prioritisation of frames the text prompt asks about.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: Whisper large-v3: word error rate 4.2% on clean audio, 12.8% on noisy audio. Latency: ~2s per minute of audio (GPU), ~15s (CPU). Cost: $0.006/minute (OpenAI Whisper API). Native audio models (GPT-4o audio, Gemini 1.5): cost ~$0.10/minute — 15x more expensive than Whisper+text. Video token costs: 1-minute video at 1fps = 60 frames, at high-res = 60 x 170 = 10,200 tokens, low-res = 60 x 85 = 5,100 tokens. Scene detection sampling: reduces to 5-10 frames for a 1-minute video (85-90% token reduction). Speaker diarization (who spoke when): additional 30% token overhead for speaker labels.

track_changes Practical Workflow: 1) For audio-only use cases, always use Whisper (or similar) for transcription — it's cheaper and more accurate than sending raw audio to a multimodal LLM. 2) Use speaker diarization (PyAnnote, AssemblyAI) to separate speakers before sending to LLM. 3) For video, detect scene changes (PySceneDetect) and sample one frame per scene — maintains narrative coherence at 10x lower cost. 4) For video understanding, use a two-stage approach: first extract text from frames (OCR) and transcript from audio, then reason over the extracted text. 5) Cache all intermediate representations (transcripts, frame embeddings) — the same video may be processed multiple times for different queries.
sections.codeBlock:
# Video processing with scene detection and transcription
import subprocess, tempfile, json
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Generator

class VideoProcessor:
    """
    Production video pipeline: extracts key frames and transcript.
    Uses scene detection for intelligent frame sampling.
    """
    def __init__(self, whisper_model="large-v3"):
        self.scene_threshold = 30.0  # scene change sensitivity

    def extract_audio(self, video_path: str, output_path: str):
        """Extract audio from video file using ffmpeg."""
        subprocess.run([
            "ffmpeg", "-i", video_path,
            "-vn", "-acodec", "pcm_s16le",
            "-ar", "16000", "-ac", "1",
            output_path, "-y"
        ], capture_output=True, check=True)

    def transcribe(self, audio_path: str) -> dict:
        """Transcribe audio with Whisper.
        Returns dict with segments, text, and speaker labels."""
        import whisper
        model = whisper.load_model(self.whisper_model)
        result = model.transcribe(audio_path, word_timestamps=True)
        return result

    def detect_scenes(self, video_path: str) -> list[dict]:
        """Detect scene changes using PySceneDetect."""
        from scenedetect import open_video, ContentDetector, SceneManager
        video = open_video(video_path)
        sm = SceneManager()
        sm.add_detector(ContentDetector(threshold=self.scene_threshold))
        sm.detect_scenes(video)
        return sm.get_scene_list()

    def extract_key_frames(self, video_path: str) -> list[tuple[float, str]]:
        """
        Extract one key frame per scene.
        Returns list of (timestamp_sec, frame_path) tuples.
        """
        scenes = self.detect_scenes(video_path)
        frames = []
        for i, (start, end) in enumerate(scenes):
            mid_time = (start.get_seconds() + end.get_seconds()) / 2
            frame_path = f"/tmp/frame_{i:04d}.jpg"
            subprocess.run([
                "ffmpeg", "-i", video_path,
                "-ss", str(mid_time),
                "-vframes", "1",
                frame_path, "-y"
            ], capture_output=True, check=True)
            frames.append((mid_time, frame_path))
        return frames

    def process_video(self, video_path: str) -> dict:
        """Full video processing pipeline."""
        with tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(suffix=".wav") as tmp_audio:
            self.extract_audio(video_path, tmp_audio.name)
            transcript = self.transcribe(tmp_audio.name)
        
        key_frames = self.extract_key_frames(video_path)
        
        return {
            "transcript": transcript["text"],
            "segments": transcript["segments"],
            "key_frames": key_frames,
            "duration_sec": transcript.get("duration", 0),
            "metadata": {
                "num_scenes": len(key_frames),
                "has_audio": len(transcript["segments"]) > 0
            }
        }

    def estimate_video_cost(self, video_path: str, detail="low") -> dict:
        """Estimate cost of video processing."""
        duration = self._get_duration(video_path)
        scenes = len(self.detect_scenes(video_path))
        tokens_per_frame = 85 if detail == "low" else 170
        image_tokens = scenes * tokens_per_frame
        audio_tokens = duration * 5  # ~5 tokens/sec audio
        total_tokens = image_tokens + audio_tokens
        return {
            "duration_sec": duration,
            "scenes": scenes,
            "image_tokens": image_tokens,
            "audio_tokens": audio_tokens,
            "total_tokens": total_tokens,
            "estimated_cost_usd": (total_tokens / 1_000_000) * 2.50
        }

    def _get_duration(self, video_path: str) -> float:
        import subprocess, json
        cmd = [
            "ffprobe", "-v", "error", "-show_entries",
            "format=duration", "-of", "json", video_path
        ]
        result = subprocess.run(cmd, capture_output=True, text=True)
        data = json.loads(result.stdout)
        return float(data["format"]["duration"])

sections.title: Multimodal Caching, Batching & Cost Optimization
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: Multimodal APIs charge per-token, and images use orders of magnitude more tokens than text. A single high-res image (~28K tokens) costs as much as ~10,000 text tokens. At scale, multimodal costs can dominate the bill. Optimization falls into three categories: caching (avoid re-encoding/re-processing the same image), batching (process multiple images in one API call), and progressive loading (start with low-res, escalate to high-res only if needed). The Staff insight: most multimodal use cases don't need full resolution — 85-90% of extraction tasks can be done with low-res mode. Reserve high-res for edge cases and retry logic.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: Image embedding caching: 60-80% cache hit rate for product catalog images (100K products, re-processed weekly). Cache saves $400/day on a $500/day vision budget. Batch processing: sending 10 images in one message vs. 10 separate messages reduces token overhead by 30% (shared system prompt, no redundant framing). Progressive low-to-high: 85% of requests succeed with low-res (85 tokens, $0.0002/image). 15% require high-res retry (28,900 tokens, $0.07/image). Average cost per image: $0.0002 + 0.15 * $0.07 = $0.011 — 6x cheaper than always using high-res. Image deduplication: near-duplicate detection (hash + perceptual hash) catches 20-30% of repeated uploads.

track_changes Practical Workflow: 1) Implement a two-level cache: (a) in-memory LRU for session-level reuse (same image in same conversation), (b) disk/Redis cache for cross-session reuse (product images, logos). 2) Use perceptual hashing (pHash) for cache keys — catches rotated/resized duplicates. 3) Implement progressive quality: each request starts with low-res. If the model returns low confidence or "can't determine", retry with high-res. 4) Batch independent image requests into a single API call. 5) Set cost budgets per user/session and alert when exceeded. 6) Log image dimensions and token usage per request for cost attribution.
sections.codeBlock:
# Multimodal caching and cost optimization
import hashlib, json, time
from collections import OrderedDict
from typing import Optional
import numpy as np

class ImageCache:
    """
    Two-level cache for image embeddings.
    L1: in-memory LRU (session). L2: disk-based (cross-session).
    """
    def __init__(self, l1_size: int = 1000, l2_path: str = "/tmp/img_cache"):
        self.l1: OrderedDict[str, dict] = OrderedDict()
        self.l1_size = l1_size
        self.l2_path = Path(l2_path)
        self.l2_path.mkdir(exist_ok=True)
        self.hits = {"l1": 0, "l2": 0}
        self.misses = 0

    def _phash(self, image: np.ndarray) -> str:
        """Perceptual hash for duplicate detection."""
        from PIL import Image
        import imagehash
        pil_img = Image.fromarray(cv2.cvtColor(image, cv2.COLOR_BGR2RGB))
        return str(imagehash.phash(pil_img))

    def get(self, image: np.ndarray) -> Optional[dict]:
        """Look up image in cache using perceptual hash."""
        key = self._phash(image)
        # Check L1
        if key in self.l1:
            self.l1.move_to_end(key)
            self.hits["l1"] += 1
            return self.l1[key]
        # Check L2
        l2_path = self.l2_path / f"{key}.json"
        if l2_path.exists():
            with open(l2_path) as f:
                data = json.load(f)
            self._add_l1(key, data)
            self.hits["l2"] += 1
            return data
        self.misses += 1
        return None

    def set(self, image: np.ndarray, data: dict):
        """Cache image data."""
        key = self._phash(image)
        self._add_l1(key, data)
        # Persist to L2
        with open(self.l2_path / f"{key}.json", "w") as f:
            json.dump(data, f)

    def _add_l1(self, key: str, data: dict):
        self.l1[key] = data
        self.l1.move_to_end(key)
        if len(self.l1) > self.l1_size:
            self.l1.popitem(last=False)

    def stats(self) -> dict:
        total = self.hits["l1"] + self.hits["l2"] + self.misses
        return {
            "l1_hits": self.hits["l1"],
            "l2_hits": self.hits["l2"],
            "misses": self.misses,
            "hit_rate": (self.hits["l1"] + self.hits["l2"]) / max(total, 1)
        }


class ProgressiveVisionProcessor:
    """
    Process images progressively: start low-res, escalate on failure.
    """
    def __init__(self, model="gpt-4o"):
        self.model = model
        self.retry_count = 0

    async def process_with_escalation(self, image: np.ndarray, prompt: str) -> dict:
        """
        Try low-res first. If low confidence, retry with high-res.
        """
        # Step 1: Low-res attempt
        low_result = await self._call_vision(image, prompt, detail="low")
        
        if self._is_confident(low_result):
            return {
                "result": low_result,
                "detail_used": "low",
                "cost": self._compute_cost("low")
            }
        
        # Step 2: Retry with high-res
        self.retry_count += 1
        high_result = await self._call_vision(image, prompt, detail="high")
        return {
            "result": high_result,
            "detail_used": "high",
            "cost": self._compute_cost("high"),
            "note": "Escalated from low-res due to low confidence"
        }

    def _is_confident(self, result: dict) -> bool:
        """Heuristic: result contains all required fields and no 'unclear'."""
        return (result.get("confidence", 1.0) > 0.8
                and "unclear" not in str(result).lower()
                and "can't determine" not in str(result).lower())

    def _compute_cost(self, detail: str) -> float:
        tokens = 85 if detail == "low" else 28900
        return (tokens / 1_000_000) * 2.50

    async def _call_vision(self, image, prompt, detail) -> dict:
        """Simulate vision API call."""
        # In production: actual API call to GPT-4o/Gemini/Claude
        await asyncio.sleep(0.1)
        return {"extracted_text": "sample", "confidence": 0.95}


class CostTracker:
    """Track multimodal API costs per user/session."""
    def __init__(self):
        self.usage: dict[str, float] = {}

    def record(self, user_id: str, model: str, tokens: int, modality: str):
        """Record a multimodal API call."""
        rates = {"gpt-4o": 2.50/1_000_000, "gpt-4": 10.00/1_000_000}
        cost = tokens * rates.get(model, 2.50/1_000_000)
        if user_id not in self.usage:
            self.usage[user_id] = 0.0
        self.usage[user_id] += cost

    def get_user_cost(self, user_id: str) -> float:
        return self.usage.get(user_id, 0.0)

    def top_users(self, n: int = 10) -> list[tuple[str, float]]:
        return sorted(self.usage.items(), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)[:n]

sections.title: Cross-Provider Multimodal Abstraction
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: Every multimodal provider has a different API format. OpenAI uses image_url content blocks with detail parameter. Anthropic uses image source blocks with base64 data. Google Gemini uses inline data or file URIs with mime_type. A cross-provider abstraction layer lets you switch models without rewriting application code. The abstraction must handle: image encoding (base64 vs URI), detail/quality settings (each provider calls it differently), token counting (different models charge different token rates), and error handling (rate limits differ by provider).

bar_chart Concrete numbers: Multi-provider fallback: if GPT-4o is down, routing to Gemini 1.5 Pro provides 95% of the accuracy for 60% of the cost. Abstraction layer overhead: ~5ms per request (Python serialisation). Provider switching time: ~500ms to failover (connection pooling + health checks). The abstraction layer handles ~30 model-specific format differences. Image size limits: OpenAI max 20MB, Anthropic max 32MB, Gemini max 20MB. Supported formats vary: OpenAI accepts PNG/JPEG/GIF/WebP, Anthropic adds BMP/TIFF, Gemini adds HEIC/HEIF.

track_changes Practical Workflow: 1) Define a unified multimodal message format: {role, content: [{type: "text"|"image", data, detail}]}. 2) Implement provider adapters that convert the unified format to each provider's native format. 3) Implement a provider selector based on: availability (health check), cost (budget-aware routing), capability (some models don't support audio), latency (fastest model for simple tasks). 4) Add circuit breakers per provider (3 failures in 60s -> fallback to next provider). 5) Log provider selection and latency per request for optimisation.

sections.title: FAANG Q&A — Multimodal LLMs
sections.description:
search Q1 (Meta E6): "Design a system that processes 100K user-uploaded images per day for content moderation." A: Three-stage pipeline. Stage 1 (pre-processing): resize to 1024px, compress JPEG 85%, compute perceptual hash for dedup, reject NSFW via fast classifier (CLIP-based). Stage 2 (LLM analysis): batch images in groups of 5 with GPT-4o low-res, extract content labels, policy violations, and confidence scores. Stage 3 (escalation): images with policy violation confidence < 0.7 get re-analysed with high-res GPT-4o. Cost: $0.01/image = $1K/day. Cache: 30% dedup saves $300/day. Total: ~$700/day for 100K images.

search Q2 (Google L6): "How do you handle video understanding where the answer requires temporal reasoning across 30 minutes of footage?" A: Hierarchical video understanding. Level 1 (scene detection): split video into scenes (typically 20-50 for 30 min). Level 2 (scene summarisation): for each scene, extract key frames and transcript, generate a one-paragraph summary. Level 3 (reasoning): feed all scene summaries as context to the LLM, which reasons across the entire video timeline. To answer time-specific queries, include start/end timestamps in each scene summary. This reduces a 30-minute video (2.7M tokens at 1fps high-res) to ~5K tokens of scene summaries.

search Q3 (Anthropic L5): "Compare the multimodal architectures of GPT-4V, Gemini 1.5 Pro, and Claude 3. Which would you pick for a document extraction use case?" A: GPT-4V: uses a ViT encoder with QK-normalised attention, supports high-res via tiling, excellent at OCR and chart understanding. Gemini 1.5 Pro: uses a MoE transformer with Mixture of Multimodal Experts, native audio/video tokenisation, best for long-context multimodal (1M tokens). Claude 3: uses vision encoder with cross-attention to text, best at visual reasoning and counting objects. For document extraction: GPT-4o is the best choice (highest DocVQA score, low cost, structured output support).

search Q4 (Apple L5): "How do you evaluate multimodal model quality for a visual Q&A use case?" A: Four-dimensional evaluation: (1) Accuracy: exact match and F1 on structured extraction tasks. (2) Hallucination rate: % of responses containing objects/details not present in the image (use negative testing — ask about objects that are NOT in the image). (3) Detail fidelity: can the model count objects correctly? (4) Spatial reasoning: can it correctly describe spatial relationships (left of, above, behind)? Use a golden dataset of 1000 labelled images with 5000 Q&A pairs. Run evaluations weekly. Track regression per model version.

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• OpenAI Vision Guide — Official documentation for GPT-4o vision capabilities, detail modes, and limitations.
  • Gemini Vision Documentation — Google's multimodal API docs covering images, audio, and video.
  • Anthropic Vision Guide — Claude's image understanding capabilities and best practices.
  • GPT-4V(ision) Technical Report (OpenAI, 2023) — Multimodal evaluation results and capability analysis.
  • Gemini 1.5 Technical Report (Google, 2024) — MoE multimodal architecture and long-context capabilities.
  • PySceneDetect: Video Scene Detection — Content-aware scene detection for intelligent frame sampling.

details:
Multimodal Decision Flow: Start with text+image (simplest, broadest support). Add pre-processing (resize, crop, compress) to reduce token costs by 4x. Use low-res detail by default, escalate to high-res on failure. For audio, use Whisper for transcription (cheaper than native audio models). For video, use scene detection sampling (10x cost reduction). Implement cross-provider abstraction for resilience and cost optimisation. Monitor cost per modality and cost per user — multimodal costs can spike 100x without guardrails. At FAANG scale, caching and progressive quality are not optional — they reduce costs by 60-80%.

Related: MCP — Model Context Protocol | Tool Use & Function Calling | LLM Observability
